title: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis
section: Protozoal and Helminthic Infections General Considerations
authors: Peter F. Weller, Edward T. Ryan

items.title: Transmission and Prevalence of Giardiasis
items.content: Giardia duodenalis is a common protozoal parasite causing intestinal disease, with prevalence rates of 20–40% in resource-constrained areas. Transmission occurs through ingestion of cysts from contaminated water or food, person-to-person contact, and poor sanitation.
keyPoints: Prevalence rates can be 20–40%, especially among young children in resource-limited settings.
Ingestion of as few as 10 cysts is sufficient to cause infection.
Transmission occurs via contaminated water, food, or person-to-person contact.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · PROTOZOAL INFECTIONS | GIARDIASIS (part 1)

items.title: Pathophysiology and Clinical Manifestations of Giardiasis
items.content: Giardia trophozoites adhere to the small intestine's epithelium, causing lactose intolerance and malabsorption due to loss of brush-border enzyme activities. The pathogenesis of diarrhea is unclear, but chronic infections can resemble tropical sprue or gluten-sensitive enteropathy.
keyPoints: Trophozoites cause apoptosis of enterocytes and epithelial barrier dysfunction.
Clinical signs include lactose intolerance and malabsorption.
Chronic infections may show flattened villi similar to tropical sprue.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · PROTOZOAL INFECTIONS | GIARDIASIS (part 1)

items.title: Natural History and Immune Response in Giardiasis
items.content: Giardia infections can be asymptomatic, transient, recurrent, or chronic. The course of infection may vary due to genotypic variations of the parasite and host factors. Humoral immune responses are crucial for controlling infection, as seen in patients with hypogammaglobulinemia.
keyPoints: Infections may be asymptomatic, transient, recurrent, or chronic.
Genotypic variations of G. duodenalis contribute to different infection courses.
Humoral immune responses are important for controlling infection.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · PROTOZOAL INFECTIONS | GIARDIASIS (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Giardiasis Clinical Manifestations
items.content: Giardiasis can present with a range of symptoms from asymptomatic carriage to severe diarrhea and malabsorption. Acute giardiasis typically develops after an incubation period of 5–6 days, usually extending to 1–3 weeks, featuring symptoms like diarrhea, abdominal pain, bloating, belching, flatus, nausea, and vomiting. Chronic giardiasis may present with intermittent or episodic symptoms such as increased flatus, loose stools, sulfurous belching, and weight loss, which can persist for years.
keyPoints: Symptoms range from asymptomatic to fulminant diarrhea and malabsorption.
Acute symptoms develop after an incubation period of 5–6 days, usually 1–3 weeks.
Common acute symptoms: diarrhea, abdominal pain, bloating, belching, flatus, nausea, vomiting.
Chronic giardiasis may present with intermittent or episodic symptoms like increased flatus, loose stools, sulfurous belching, and weight loss.
Fever, blood/mucus in stools, colitis signs suggest a different diagnosis.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · GIARDIASIS (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic Approaches for Giardiasis
items.content: Diagnosis of giardiasis involves detecting parasite antigens in feces, identifying cysts or trophozoites, and using nucleic acid amplification tests (NAATs). Cysts are oval, measuring 8–12 μm × 7–10 μm with four nuclei, while trophozoites are pear-shaped with two nuclei and four pairs of flagella. Due to variable cyst excretion, repeated stool examinations or duodenal fluid sampling may be necessary.
keyPoints: Diagnosis by detecting parasite antigens in feces, identifying cysts/trophozoites, or using NAATs.
Cysts are oval (8–12 μm × 7–10 μm) with four nuclei; trophozoites are pear-shaped with two nuclei and four pairs of flagella.
Direct stool examination, concentration methods, duodenal fluid sampling, small intestine biopsy may be needed due to variable cyst excretion.
Antigen tests in stool often more sensitive than microscopic examination, easier to perform, rapid turn-around time.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · GIARDIASIS (part 2)

items.title: Treatment Efficacy in Giardiasis
items.content: Metronidazole is highly effective for treating giardiasis, with cure rates usually exceeding 90%. Tinidazole may offer greater efficacy than metronidazole. For patients who do not respond to initial treatments or have chronic symptoms, prolonged therapy or varied combinations of multiple agents can be considered.
keyPoints: Metronidazole (250–500 mg thrice daily for 5 days) has cure rates usually >90%.
Tinidazole may be more effective than metronidazole.
Prolonged therapy with metronidazole (750 mg thrice daily for 21 days) or varied combinations of agents can be successful in refractory cases.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Giardiasis | CRYPTOSPORIDIOSIS

items.title: Prevention Strategies for Giardiasis
items.content: Giardiasis prevention focuses on consuming uncontaminated food and water, maintaining personal hygiene, especially when caring for infected children. Boiling or filtering potentially contaminated water is effective in preventing infection.
keyPoints: Prevent giardiasis by ensuring consumption of uncontaminated food and water.
Maintain personal hygiene during care for infected individuals.
Boil or filter potentially contaminated water to prevent infection.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Giardiasis | CRYPTOSPORIDIOSIS

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Diagnosis of Cryptosporidiosis in Immunocompromised Hosts
items.content: In immunocompromised hosts, particularly those with AIDS and CD4+ T-cell counts <100/μL, cryptosporidiosis can cause chronic, persistent diarrhea that is profuse and leads to significant fluid and electrolyte depletion. Diagnosis involves fecal examination using specific techniques like modified acid-fast staining or enzyme immunoassays.
keyPoints: In immunocompromised hosts, diarrhea can be chronic and profuse with stool volumes ranging from 1 to 25 L/d.
CD4+ T-cell counts <100/μL in AIDS patients increase the risk of severe symptoms.
Diagnosis requires specific testing methods such as modified acid-fast staining or enzyme immunoassays.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Giardiasis | CRYPTOSPORIDIOSIS

items.title: Nitazoxanide Treatment for Cryptosporidiosis in Immunocompetent Patients
items.content: Nitazoxanide is FDA-approved for treating cryptosporidiosis in immunocompetent adults and children, with specific dosing guidelines. It is ineffective in immunosuppressed patients, where improved immune status from antiretroviral therapy may help.
keyPoints: FDA-approved treatment: Nitazoxanide
Adult dosage: 500 mg twice daily for 3 days
Child dosage: Available as an elixir
Ineffective in immunosuppressed or HIV-infected patients
Supportive care includes fluids, electrolytes, and antidiarrheal agents
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cryptosporidiosis | CYSTOISOSPORIASIS

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Cystoisosporiasis in Immunocompromised Patients
items.content: Cystoisosporiasis, caused by C. belli, presents with fever, abdominal pain, and watery diarrhea, especially in immunocompromised patients. Diagnosis involves detecting large oocysts in stool using modified acid-fast staining or NAATs.
keyPoints: Caused by: Cystoisospora belli
Common symptoms: Fever, abdominal pain, watery nonbloody diarrhea
Chronic diarrhea in immunocompromised patients
Eosinophilia may be present in immunocompetent hosts
Oocyst size: ~25 µm
Diagnostic methods: Modified acid-fast staining, NAATs
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cryptosporidiosis | CYSTOISOSPORIASIS

items.title: Treatment and Maintenance for Cystoisosporiasis in HIV-Infected Patients
items.content: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) is effective for treating cystoisosporiasis, with a regimen of 160/800 mg twice daily for 7–10 days. For HIV-infected patients, maintenance therapy involves continuing TMP-SMX three times weekly due to the risk of relapse.
keyPoints: Effective treatment: TMP-SMX 160/800 mg two times daily for 7–10 days
HIV-infected patients require continuation at 160/800 mg three times per week
Alternative treatments include pyrimethamine (50–75 mg/d) or ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice a day
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cystoisosporiasis | CYCLOSPORIASIS

items.title: Diagnosis and Clinical Features of Cyclosporiasis
items.content: Cyclospora cayetanensis is associated with prolonged diarrhea, flulike symptoms, and gastrointestinal discomfort. Diagnosis involves detecting 8- to 10-$ \mu $m oocysts in stool samples using specific fecal examinations or NAATs.
keyPoints: Clinical features: Diarrhea, flulike symptoms, flatulence, belching
Diarrheal illness may persist for >1 month
Oocyst size: 8- to 10-$ \mu $m
Detection methods: Specific fecal examinations or NAATs
Consider in differential diagnosis of prolonged diarrhea
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cystoisosporiasis | CYCLOSPORIASIS

items.title: Treatment of Cyclosporiasis and Microsporidiosis in HIV-Infected Patients
items.content: Cyclosporiasis is treated with TMP-SMX, while microsporidiosis treatment varies based on the species involved. In HIV-infected patients, cyclosporiasis may require longer courses or maintenance therapy due to potential relapses.
keyPoints: TMP-SMX (160/800 mg twice daily for 7–10 days) is the standard treatment for Cyclosporiasis.
HIV-infected patients with Cyclosporiasis may need extended treatment and suppressive maintenance therapy.
Nitazoxanide and ciprofloxacin are alternatives if TMP-SMX is contraindicated.
Microsporidiosis in HIV involves Enterocytozoon bieneusi and Encephalitozoon species, causing chronic diarrhea and wasting.
Enterocytozoon bieneusi and Encephalitozoon intestinalis found in 10–40% of AIDS patients with chronic diarrhea.
Superficial keratoconjunctivitis due to E. hellem can be treated with topical fumagillin suspension.
Albendazole may be effective for enteric infections with E. intestinalis in HIV-infected patients.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cyclosporiasis | MICROSPORIDIOSIS

items.title: Diagnosis and Clinical Manifestations of Microsporidiosis
items.content: Microsporidiosis diagnosis often requires electron microscopy, but light microscopy can visualize spores. It manifests differently in immunocompromised patients, with specific species causing distinct clinical syndromes.
keyPoints: Microsporidia are small gram-positive organisms with mature spores measuring 0.5–2 µm × 1–4 µm.
Diagnosis often requires electron microscopy; light microscopy can visualize spores with hematoxylin and eosin, Giemsa, or Gram's stain.
Light microscopy with modified trichrome stain or fluorescent assays can be used on stool, tissue, or urine.
NAATs are useful for diagnosis and speciation of microsporidiosis.
Microsporidiosis is common in AIDS patients, causing chronic diarrhea and wasting.
E. intestinalis may disseminate to cause fever, diarrhea, sinusitis, cholangitis, and bronchiolitis.
Encephalitozoon hellem can cause superficial keratoconjunctivitis and respiratory tract disease.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cyclosporiasis | MICROSPORIDIOSIS

items.title: Balantidiasis: Transmission, Pathology, and Treatment
items.content: Balantidium coli causes intestinal disease similar to amebiasis. It is transmitted through cysts from pigs or contaminated water. Infection can lead to asymptomatic carriage, intermittent diarrhea, or severe dysentery. Diagnosis involves detecting the parasite in stool or colonic tissue.
keyPoints: Balantidium coli infects pigs and humans, especially where pigs are raised.
Transmission occurs via cysts from person-to-person, water, or pig feces.
Symptoms range from asymptomatic to severe dysentery with mucosal invasion.
Diagnosis is by stool or colonic tissue examination.
Tetracycline (500 mg four times daily for 10 days) and metronidazole are effective treatments.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · OTHER INTESTINAL PROTOZOA

items.title: Blastocystosis: Uncertain Pathogenicity and Management
items.content: Blastocystis hominis is of uncertain pathogenicity. Some patients are asymptomatic, while others experience diarrhea and intestinal symptoms. Due to its uncertain role in causing disease, thorough evaluation for other causes is recommended before treatment.
keyPoints: Pathogenicity of Blastocystis hominis is uncertain.
Symptoms include diarrhea; differential diagnosis should rule out other pathogens.
Metronidazole (500–750 mg thrice daily for 5–10 days) or tinidazole (2 g once) can be used if symptoms are prominent.
Alternative treatments include paromomycin, TMP-SMX, and nitazoxanide.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · OTHER INTESTINAL PROTOZOA

items.title: Dientamoebiasis: Diagnosis and Treatment
items.content: Dientamoeba fragilis causes mild gastrointestinal symptoms. It lacks a cyst stage, making transmission unclear. Diagnosis is through stool examination for trophozoites, with NAATs being more sensitive than microscopy.
keyPoints: Dientamoeba fragilis has only a trophozoite stage.
Symptoms include intermittent diarrhea, abdominal pain, and anorexia.
Diagnosis by stool detection of trophozoites; NAATs are more sensitive.
Treatment includes paromomycin (25–35 mg/kg per day for 7 days) or metronidazole (500–750 mg three times daily for 10 days).
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · OTHER INTESTINAL PROTOZOA

items.title: Epidemiology and Transmission of Trichomoniasis
items.content: Trichomonas vaginalis is a prevalent protozoal parasite in the United States, primarily transmitted through person-to-person venereal contact. It affects both genders but has higher symptomatic rates in women.
keyPoints: 2–3 million infections each year in the U.S.
Prevalence highest among persons with multiple sexual partners and those with other sexually transmitted diseases
Person-to-person venereal transmission accounts for virtually all cases
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · TRICHOMONIASIS

items.title: Diagnosis of Trichomoniasis
items.content: Trichomoniasis diagnosis involves detecting motile trichomonads, with NAATs being the most sensitive and specific method for both urine and swabs.
keyPoints: Sensitivity of wet-mount examination is ~50–60%
Direct immunofluorescent antibody staining sensitivity is 70–90%
NAATs are highly sensitive and specific, often considered first-line diagnostics
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · TRICHOMONIASIS

items.title: Treatment Options for Trichomoniasis
items.content: Metronidazole, tinidazole, and secnidazole are effective treatments for trichomoniasis. Concurrent treatment of all sexual partners is crucial to prevent reinfection.
keyPoints: Effective treatments include metronidazole (2-g single dose or 500-mg twice daily for 7 days), tinidazole (single 2-g dose), and secnidazole (single 2-g dose).
All sexual partners must be treated concurrently to prevent reinfection, particularly from asymptomatic males.
In males with persistent symptomatic urethritis after therapy for nongonococcal urethritis, metronidazole should be considered.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Trichomoniasis

items.title: Managing Metronidazole-Resistant Trichomoniasis
items.content: For trichomoniasis strains resistant to metronidazole, higher oral or parenteral doses of metronidazole, concurrent oral and vaginal dosing, or tinidazole can be effective.
keyPoints: Reinfection is a common cause of apparent treatment failure; however, high-level resistance to metronidazole has been reported.
Treatment options for resistant infections include higher oral doses, parenteral doses, concurrent oral and vaginal dosing of metronidazole, or tinidazole.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Trichomoniasis

items.question: Which stage of Giardia is responsible for transmission outside the human body?
items.explanation: The resistant cyst stage of Giardia survives outside the body and is responsible for transmission.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · PROTOZOAL INFECTIONS | GIARDIASIS (part 1)

items.question: What conditions enhance the efficacy of water as a means of transmitting Giardia?
options: Rapid desiccation of cysts in cold water
Resistance of cysts to routine chlorination methods
items.explanation: Cysts are resistant to killing by routine chlorination, enhancing waterborne transmission.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · PROTOZOAL INFECTIONS | GIARDIASIS (part 1)

items.question: Which genotypes of G. duodenalis commonly infect humans?
items.explanation: Human infections are due to Giardia assemblages A and B.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · PROTOZOAL INFECTIONS | GIARDIASIS (part 1)

items.question: What is a clinical sign of the loss of epithelial brush-border enzyme activities in giardiasis?
items.explanation: Consequent lactose intolerance is a clinical sign of the loss of epithelial brush-border enzyme activities.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · PROTOZOAL INFECTIONS | GIARDIASIS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common symptom of acute giardiasis?
items.explanation: Fever, blood and/or mucus in stools are uncommon symptoms of giardiasis.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · GIARDIASIS (part 2)

items.question: What is the typical incubation period for acute giardiasis?
items.explanation: The incubation period for acute giardiasis lasts at least 5–6 days and usually 1–3 weeks.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · GIARDIASIS (part 2)

items.question: Which diagnostic method is often the first-line test in many institutions for detecting Giardia?
options: Tests for parasitic antigens in stool
items.explanation: Tests for parasitic antigens in stool are often more sensitive, easier to perform, and have a rapid turn-around time.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · GIARDIASIS (part 2)

items.question: Which treatment for giardiasis is reported to have a cure rate greater than 90%?
items.explanation: Cure rates with metronidazole are usually greater than 90%.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Giardiasis | CRYPTOSPORIDIOSIS

items.question: What is an alternative treatment for giardiasis in symptomatic pregnant patients?
items.explanation: Paromomycin can be given to symptomatic pregnant patients.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Giardiasis | CRYPTOSPORIDIOSIS

items.question: Which Cryptosporidium species are most commonly responsible for human infections?
items.explanation: C. hominis and C. parvum cause most human infections.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Giardiasis | CRYPTOSPORIDIOSIS

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of Cryptosporidium infection in immunocompromised hosts?
options: Diarrhea can be chronic and profuse.
Cryptosporidia are found only in the small bowel.
items.explanation: In immunocompromised hosts, diarrhea can be chronic, persistent, and remarkably profuse.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Giardiasis | CRYPTOSPORIDIOSIS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT an effective treatment for cryptosporidiosis in immunosuppressed patients?
options: Antiretroviral therapy leading to improved immune status
Supportive care with fluids and electrolytes
items.explanation: Nitazoxanide is not effective for the treatment of cryptosporidiosis in immunosuppressed patients.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cryptosporidiosis | CYSTOISOSPORIASIS

items.question: What is a recommended preventive measure to minimize acquisition of cryptosporidiosis from drinking water?
items.explanation: Use of submicron water filters may minimize acquisition of cryptosporidiosis from drinking water.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cryptosporidiosis | CYSTOISOSPORIASIS

items.question: Which diagnostic method is used to detect Cystoisospora belli oocysts in stool samples?
items.explanation: The diagnosis of Cystoisospora belli is usually made by detection of the large oocysts in stool by modified acid-fast staining.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cryptosporidiosis | CYSTOISOSPORIASIS

items.question: Which medication regimen is recommended for treating Cystoisosporiasis in HIV-infected patients?
options: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) 160/800 mg two times daily for 7–10 days, then continuing three times daily for 3–4 weeks
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg once a day for 2 weeks
items.explanation: TMP-SMX is effective and should be continued three times daily for 3–4 weeks in HIV-infected patients.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cystoisosporiasis | CYCLOSPORIASIS

items.question: Which of the following statements about Cyclospora cayetanensis transmission is correct?
options: It is primarily transmitted through airborne particles.
Waterborne and foodborne transmissions have been recognized.
Transmission occurs only through direct person-to-person contact.
There is no known mode of transmission.
items.explanation: Cyclospora cayetanensis has waterborne and foodborne transmission routes.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cystoisosporiasis | CYCLOSPORIASIS

items.question: How is Cyclospora diagnosed?
options: Through detection of spherical 8- to 10-$ \mu $m oocysts in the stool using specific fecal examinations.
Via blood tests for antibodies against Cyclospora.
Using endoscopy and biopsy of the small intestine.
items.explanation: Specific fecal examinations are required to detect variably acid-fast, fluorescent oocysts in stool samples.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cystoisosporiasis | CYCLOSPORIASIS

items.question: What is the first-line treatment for cyclosporiasis?
options: TMP-SMX (160/800 mg twice daily for 7–10 days)
items.explanation: TMP-SMX is the first-line treatment for cyclosporiasis.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cyclosporiasis | MICROSPORIDIOSIS

items.question: Which microsporidia species are most commonly associated with chronic diarrhea in patients with AIDS?
items.explanation: Enterocytozoon bieneusi and Encephalitozoon intestinalis are associated with chronic diarrhea in AIDS patients.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cyclosporiasis | MICROSPORIDIOSIS

items.question: Which diagnostic method can be used to visualize intracellular spores of microsporidia?
options: Light microscopy with hematoxylin and eosin
items.explanation: Intracellular spores can be visualized by light microscopy with hematoxylin and eosin.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cyclosporiasis | MICROSPORIDIOSIS

items.question: Which protozoan parasite associated with balantidiasis can cause a spectrum of large-intestinal disease similar to amebiasis?
items.explanation: Balantidium coli is the protozoan parasite associated with balantidiasis that can cause large-intestinal disease similar to amebiasis.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · OTHER INTESTINAL PROTOZOA

items.question: What is a common route of transmission for Balantidium coli infection in humans?
options: Ingestion of cysts from porcine feces
items.explanation: Balantidium coli is commonly transmitted through ingestion of cysts derived from porcine feces.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · OTHER INTESTINAL PROTOZOA

items.question: Which treatment is considered an effective therapeutic agent for balantidiasis?
items.explanation: Tetracycline (500 mg four times daily for 10 days) is an effective therapeutic agent for balantidiasis.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · OTHER INTESTINAL PROTOZOA

items.question: What distinguishes Dientamoeba fragilis from other intestinal protozoa?
options: It is transmitted by airborne droplets
items.explanation: Dientamoeba fragilis is unique among intestinal protozoa in that it has a trophozoite stage but not a cyst stage.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · OTHER INTESTINAL PROTOZOA

items.question: Which of the following is a major cause of symptomatic vaginitis in the United States?
items.explanation: Trichomonas vaginalis is a major cause of symptomatic vaginitis.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · TRICHOMONIASIS

items.question: What is the typical incubation period for Trichomonas vaginalis infection in women?
items.explanation: The incubation period for T. vaginalis infection in women is 5–28 days.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · TRICHOMONIASIS

items.question: Which diagnostic method has a sensitivity of ~50-60% for detecting Trichomonas vaginalis in routine evaluations?
items.explanation: Microscopic examination of wet mounts has a sensitivity of ~50–60% for detecting T. vaginalis.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · TRICHOMONIASIS

items.question: Which medication is NOT mentioned as an effective treatment for trichomoniasis?
items.explanation: Azithromycin is not listed as a treatment option for trichomoniasis in the passage.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Trichomoniasis

items.question: What should be done to prevent reinfection of trichomoniasis?
items.explanation: All sexual partners must be treated concurrently to prevent reinfection.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Trichomoniasis

items.question: Which treatment strategy has been successful for metronidazole-resistant trichomoniasis?
options: Higher oral or parenteral doses, or concurrent oral and vaginal doses
Switching to antibiotics targeting bacterial infections
items.explanation: Treatment with higher oral doses, parenteral doses, or concurrent oral and vaginal doses has been successful for resistant infections.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Trichomoniasis

items.statement: Giardia cysts can survive for months in cold fresh water.
items.explanation: Cysts remain viable for months in cold fresh water.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · PROTOZOAL INFECTIONS | GIARDIASIS (part 1)

items.explanation: Giardia remains a pathogen of the proximal small bowel and does not disseminate hematogenously.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · PROTOZOAL INFECTIONS | GIARDIASIS (part 1)

items.statement: Giardiasis can cause severe illness in patients with hypogammaglobulinemia.
items.explanation: Giardiasis is known to be more severe in patients with hypogammaglobulinemia.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · GIARDIASIS (part 2)

items.statement: Diarrhea is always the most prominent symptom of giardiasis.
items.explanation: While diarrhea is common, upper intestinal symptoms like nausea and abdominal pain may predominate.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · GIARDIASIS (part 2)

items.statement: Cryptosporidium oocysts are effectively killed by routine chlorination.
items.explanation: Oocysts resist killing by routine chlorination.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Giardiasis | CRYPTOSPORIDIOSIS

items.statement: Cryptosporidiosis is the most common cause of moderate to severe diarrhea in children under two years old.
items.explanation: Rotavirus, not cryptosporidiosis, is the most common cause.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Giardiasis | CRYPTOSPORIDIOSIS

items.statement: Cystoisospora belli infections are self-limited in immunocompetent hosts.
items.explanation: Infections with C. belli often resolve on their own in immunocompetent individuals.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cryptosporidiosis | CYSTOISOSPORIASIS

items.statement: Eosinophilia is commonly found in cryptosporidiosis.
items.explanation: Eosinophilia is not typically associated with cryptosporidiosis but may be seen in Cystoisospora belli infections.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cryptosporidiosis | CYSTOISOSPORIASIS

items.statement: Diarrheal illness due to Cyclospora can persist for more than one month.
items.explanation: The passage states that diarrheal illness may persist for >1 month.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cystoisosporiasis | CYCLOSPORIASIS

items.statement: Disease due to Cyclospora is caused by destruction of the small-bowel mucosa.
items.explanation: The absence of fecal blood and leukocytes indicates that disease due to Cyclospora does not involve destruction of the small-bowel mucosa.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cystoisosporiasis | CYCLOSPORIASIS

items.statement: Microsporidia are obligate intracellular spore-forming protozoa.
items.explanation: Microsporidia are indeed obligate intracellular spore-forming protozoa.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cyclosporiasis | MICROSPORIDIOSIS

items.statement: Albendazole is the definitive therapy for all microsporidial infections.
items.explanation: Definitive therapies for microsporidial infections remain to be established; albendazole is used specifically for enteric infections with E. intestinalis in HIV-infected patients.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Cyclosporiasis | MICROSPORIDIOSIS

items.statement: Blastocystosis caused by Blastocystis hominis is always associated with severe symptoms.
items.explanation: Some patients who pass B. hominis in their stools are asymptomatic, while others have diarrhea and associated intestinal symptoms.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · OTHER INTESTINAL PROTOZOA

items.statement: Sarcocystosis can lead to transient myositis with peripheral eosinophilia following ingestion of oocysts.
items.explanation: Humans can develop a transient myositis with peripheral eosinophilia following ingestion of oocysts of S. nesbitti shed by snakes.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · OTHER INTESTINAL PROTOZOA

items.statement: Trichomonas vaginalis can be detected in the urethra of both males and females.
items.explanation: T. vaginalis can indeed be recovered from the urethra of both males and females.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · TRICHOMONIASIS

items.statement: NAATs are less sensitive than wet-mount examinations for diagnosing trichomoniasis.
items.explanation: NAATs are more sensitive and specific compared to wet-mount examinations.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · TRICHOMONIASIS

items.statement: Metronidazole resistance in T. vaginalis is not a concern.
items.explanation: Strains of T. vaginalis exhibiting high-level resistance to metronidazole have been encountered.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Trichomoniasis

items.statement: Alternatives to metronidazole for trichomoniasis treatment during pregnancy are readily available.
items.explanation: Alternatives to metronidazole for treatment during pregnancy are not readily available.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Trichomoniasis

items.assertion: The ingestion of as few as 10 Giardia cysts is sufficient to cause infection in humans.
items.reason: Cysts are infectious when excreted and person-to-person transmission occurs where fecal hygiene is poor.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why ingestion of as few as 10 cysts can cause infection.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · PROTOZOAL INFECTIONS | GIARDIASIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Giardia infections may be asymptomatic or chronic.
items.reason: The natural history of Giardia infection varies markedly due to genotypic variations in the parasites.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why the course of infection can vary.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · PROTOZOAL INFECTIONS | GIARDIASIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Cryptosporidium infection can be transmitted through water.
items.reason: Oocysts are hardy and resist routine chlorination, leading to waterborne transmission.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Giardiasis | CRYPTOSPORIDIOSIS

items.assertion: Cryptosporidiosis causes characteristic pathologic changes in intestinal biopsies.
items.reason: The parasite's development involves both asexual and sexual cycles within the host.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; no characteristic pathologic changes are found by biopsy.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Giardiasis | CRYPTOSPORIDIOSIS

items.assertion: Balantidiasis can spread hematogenously to other organs.
items.reason: Balantidiasis, unlike amebiasis, only rarely spreads hematogenously to other organs.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; balantidiasis rarely spreads hematogenously.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · OTHER INTESTINAL PROTOZOA

items.assertion: Dientamoeba fragilis infection can be diagnosed by detection of trophozoites in stool.
items.reason: The lability of trophozoites accounts for the greater yield when fecal samples are preserved immediately after collection.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why immediate preservation aids diagnosis.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · OTHER INTESTINAL PROTOZOA

items.assertion: Reinfection is a common cause of apparent treatment failures in trichomoniasis.
items.reason: Strains of T. vaginalis exhibiting high-level resistance to metronidazole have been encountered.
items.explanation: While reinfection often accounts for apparent treatment failures, the reason given pertains to drug resistance rather than reinfection.
items.reference: Protozoal Intestinal Infections and Trichomoniasis · Trichomoniasis
